figures.caption: Table 14: Participant-ID corruption test on GRASP-Bench. We consistently remap Person IDs in the question, answer options, and metadata answer text while keeping the video unchanged and preserving the correct answer letter. Normal and Corrupt denote accuracy (%), and Drop denotes Corrupt minus Normal in percentage points.
Figure 14: Error profile for SGR compared against difficulty of the questions for the base models.
Figure 13: Accuracy compared against average reasoning length for all baselines.

Figure 9: QA category distribution. The benchmark contains 16 categories: six gaze categories (T1–T6), six gesture categories (G1–G6), and four joint reasoning categories (J1–J4). Bar colors indicate modality, and markers indicate difficulty.

Figure 10: Data construction yield. The pipeline starts from source videos, detects raw gaze and gesture events, filters high-confidence gaze events, merges timeline events, and generates QA pairs and per-QA video clips for training.
Figure 18: Qualitative comparison on MMSI speaker target identification (STI).
Figure 17: Qualitative comparison on a hard GRASP-Bench example.

Figure 1: Example from GRASP. Multi-person social reasoning requires grounding subtle non-verbal cues in the correct participants over time. Existing MLLMs [19, 78] often take spurious scene-level shortcuts, whereas ours leverage evidence-aware supervision to reason from the relevant social event.
Table 1: Evaluation results on GRASP-Bench. We report accuracy for all 16 categories. Columns are grouped by reasoning type and difficulty level.
Figure 15: Qualitative comparison on an easy GRASP-Bench example.
Figure 16: Qualitative comparison on a medium GRASP-Bench example.
Figure 11: Average gaze and gesture event density per video. Social Deduction Game has the highest event density due to continuous multi-person social interaction.
Figure 23: Qualitative GRASP-Bench examples for gaze reasoning, covering T1–T6.

Figure 22: Failure cases on GRASP-Bench. We show two representative errors: an ambiguous deictic gesture where reaching and pointing cues are visually close, and a crowded gaze-reasoning case where multiple gaze events occur within the target interval.
Figure 24: Qualitative GRASP-Bench examples for gesture reasoning, covering G1–G6.
Figure 25: Qualitative GRASP-Bench examples for joint gaze–gesture reasoning, covering J1–J4.

Table 10: Task examples in our 16-category social-reasoning benchmark. Each row shows one representative MCQ-style question–answer pair (only the correct answer text is shown for brevity). Difficulty progresses Easy → Hard along the rows within each modality (T1–T2 Easy, T3–T4 Medium, T5–T6 Hard; analogous for G/J).

Figure 12: Distribution of MCQ training examples by modality and difficulty. Open-ended examples are excluded from this heatmap because they are used for SFT and are categorized as hard reasoning examples.

Table 12: Comparison of GRASP with existing multimodal social understanding benchmarks. Our dataset scales significantly beyond prior works while distinctly focusing on fine-grained, participantlevel non-verbal grounding.
Table 2: Cross-benchmark generalization results on MMSI, Online-MMSI, and TVQA+. MMSI and Online-MMSI are evaluated on three social reasoning tasks: STI, PCR, and MPP.

Figure 2: Overview of the GRASP construction pipeline and QA examples. We convert multi-person videos into person-consistent gaze and gesture events, compose them into structured social QA pairs, and apply subset validation with human feedback for quality control.
Figure 26: Prompt for deictic gesture annotation.

Table 11: Human validation results on GRASP-Bench. Four evaluators audit approximately 25% of the benchmark in a category-balanced manner. We report the percentage of QA instances judged visually supported, temporally grounded, and unambiguous.

Figure 6: Human validation interface. Evaluators inspect each QA instance with the corresponding video clip, participant IDs, temporal context, category label, question, and answer options, then verify whether the QA pair is visually grounded and unambiguous.
Table 8: Gesture types annotated in GRASP and their conservative annotation criteria.
Figure 21: Qualitative comparison on TVQA+.
Table 13: Detailed training hyperparameters.
Figure 5: Grounded participant precision—accuracy on GRASP-Bench across various reasoning baselines. Marker size reflects the average number of novel participants mentioned in the reasoning trace.
Figure 27: Prompt QA generation.
Figure 19: Qualitative comparison on MMSI pronoun coreference resolution (PCR).
Figure 20: Qualitative comparison on MMSI mentioned-player prediction (MPP).
Figure 8: Distribution of retained gaze event types and gesture types. Gaze events are filtered at confidence ≥ 0.9, and gestures are filtered at confidence ≥ 0.85.

Figure 7: Dataset-level scale across the six source domains. We report the number of source videos, retained gaze events, detected gestures, and generated QA pairs for each dataset. The y-axis is shown in log scale to make datasets of different sizes comparable.
